flashcards.front: What is the function of the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) in a CPU?
flashcards.back: The ALU performs arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, comparison) on binary data.

flashcards.front: Define the instruction cycle and list its main phases.
flashcards.back: The instruction cycle is the process by which a CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions. Main phases: Fetch, Decode, Execute, and Store.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between RISC and CISC architectures?
flashcards.back: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) uses simple, fixed-length instructions with few addressing modes. CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) uses complex, variable-length instructions with many addressing modes.

flashcards.front: Explain the concept of interrupt-driven I/O.
flashcards.back: Interrupt-driven I/O allows devices to notify the CPU when they need attention by sending an interrupt signal, instead of the CPU continuously polling the device status.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Control Unit (CU) in a CPU?
flashcards.back: The Control Unit directs the operation of the processor by issuing control signals that coordinate the fetching, decoding, and execution of instructions.

flashcards.front: Define memory hierarchy and list its levels from fastest to slowest.
flashcards.back: Memory hierarchy organizes storage based on speed and cost. From fastest to slowest: Registers, Cache (L1, L2, L3), Main Memory (RAM), Secondary Storage (HDD, SSD).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between memory-mapped I/O and isolated I/O?
flashcards.back: Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address space for memory and I/O devices, treating I/O devices as memory locations. Isolated I/O uses separate address spaces and special instructions for I/O operations.

flashcards.front: Explain the role of the system bus in computer architecture.
flashcards.back: The system bus connects CPU, memory, and I/O devices. It consists of address bus (carries memory addresses), data bus (carries data), and control bus (carries control signals).
DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from main memory without CPU intervention, freeing the CPU to perform other tasks during data transfer.

flashcards.front: Define the concept of dual-mode operation in operating systems.
flashcards.back: Dual-mode operation provides two modes: User Mode (restricted privileges) and Kernel Mode (full privileges). The CPU switches between modes based on the type of operation being performed.

flashcards.front: What is the function of cache memory?
flashcards.back: Cache memory is a small, fast memory that stores frequently accessed data and instructions closer to the CPU, reducing the time needed to access data from main memory.

flashcards.front: Explain what a system call is and its purpose.
flashcards.back: A system call is a request made by a user program to the operating system kernel for privileged operations like file handling, process management, and memory allocation.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Program Counter (PC) register?
flashcards.back: The Program Counter (PC) holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched and executed by the CPU.

flashcards.front: Define booting and explain the bootstrap loader.
flashcards.back: Booting is the process of starting a computer. The bootstrap loader is a small program stored in ROM that initializes the system and loads the operating system into memory.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous I/O?
flashcards.back: Synchronous I/O blocks the requesting process until the I/O operation completes. Asynchronous I/O allows the process to continue executing while I/O proceeds in the background.
